claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by understanding your child's situation.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

- Is your child **under 16 years old**?
- Is your child **between 16 and 19 years old**?
- Is your child **20 years old or over**?

Please let me know your child's age category, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please tell me about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me check the eligibility for Alex, who is 8 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, that's a good start. Now I need to check a few more things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex is only 8 years old, they wouldn't be working full-time. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

Is Alex **NOT** receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(For an 8-year-old, this would typically be "yes" - they're not receiving these benefits)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship? 

(For an 8-year-old, this would be "no" - they're too young for apprenticeships)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about **your residency status** as the claimant:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This is one of the key requirements for claiming Child Benefit. There are several ways to meet the residency requirement, including:
- Normally living in the UK
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions)
- Being a Crown servant living abroad

So, do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? This means either:
- You live with Alex, OR
- You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying for their food, clothes, housing, etc. - at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount)

Do you live with Alex or contribute to their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. I do help out a bit with things like clothes and school supplies - I give about £10 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me check the current Child Benefit rates to see if your contribution meets the requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Currently, you're contributing about £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep. 

To meet the "responsibility" requirement through financial contributions, you need to contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit payment amount. For one child (the eldest or only child), the current Child Benefit rate is approximately **£25.60 per week** (though you should check gov.uk for the exact current rate).

Your contribution of £10 per week is below this threshold, and you've indicated that Alex doesn't live with you.

**Important question:** Is there someone else who Alex lives with and who is primarily responsible for their care (such as Alex's other parent, a guardian, or another family member)? 

This is important because that person might be the one who should claim Child Benefit for Alex, as they would meet the "living with the child" requirement.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is an important situation to understand.

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner, and you contribute £10 per week (which is below the Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week), **your ex-partner would be the person who is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**, not you.

Here's why:
- **Responsibility requirement**: To claim Child Benefit, you must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep
- You don't live with Alex
- Your contribution (£10/week) is below the required threshold (approximately £25.60/week)
- Your ex-partner lives with Alex, which automatically meets the responsibility requirement

Let me navigate to the appropriate outcome based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Result

**Result: NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

### Why You're Not Eligible:

You must be **responsible for the child** to claim Child Benefit. This means you need to either:
1. **Live with the child**, OR
2. **Contribute at least the Child Benefit amount** towards their care (currently approximately £25.60 per week for one child)

In your situation:
- Alex does not live with you (Alex lives with your ex-partner)
- You contribute £10 per week, which is below the required threshold of approximately £25.60 per week

### What This Means:

**Your ex-partner should claim Child Benefit for Alex**, as they live with Alex and are providing day-to-day care. Living with the child automatically satisfies the responsibility requirement.

### Your Options:

1. **If you want to claim Child Benefit**: You would need to increase your financial contributions to at least the Child Benefit rate (approximately £25.60/week) towards Alex's upkeep. This can include money for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, or other care expenses.

2. **If your ex-partner hasn't claimed**: They should apply for Child Benefit, as they are the eligible claimant.

3. **Child maintenance**: Your £10/week contribution is separate from Child Benefit eligibility. If you have a child maintenance arrangement, that continues regardless of who claims Child Benefit.

For more information, visit: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). However, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement for Child Benefit. Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant's financial contribution of £10 per week is below the Child Benefit rate threshold of approximately £25.60 per week. To claim Child Benefit through financial contributions alone, the claimant must pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards Alex's care. Since neither condition is met (not living with the child AND not contributing the required amount), the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for their child Alex because they do not meet the responsibility requirement. While Alex meets all the child-related criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship), and the claimant meets the UK residency requirement, the claimant does not live with Alex and their financial contribution (£10/week) falls below the required threshold. Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, who would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant's financial contribution of £10 per week is below the Child Benefit rate threshold of approximately £25.60 per week. To claim Child Benefit through financial contributions alone, the claimant must pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards Alex's care. Since neither condition is met (not living with the child AND not contributing the required amount), the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for their child Alex because they do not meet the responsibility requirement. While Alex meets all the child-related criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship), and the claimant meets the UK residency requirement, the claimant does not live with Alex and their financial contribution (£10/week) falls below the required threshold.
